data.title: Canada's Goodfood Market Q2 net sales fall 26%, net loss widens
data.description:
Overview

  • Canada meal solutions provider's fiscal Q2 net sales fell 26% yr/yr amid CFIA suspension

  • Gross margin dropped 12 percentage points due to higher costs from CFIA suspension

  • Net loss widened to C$6.8 mln, impacted by lower sales and reorganization costs

Outlook

  • Company is focused on improving unit economics and generating consistent cash flow

  • Goodfood plans selective expansion into adjacent food categories to support growth

  • Management remains focused on strengthening the balance sheet and maintaining capital flexibility

Result Drivers

  • CFIA SUSPENSION - Temporary CFIA license suspension reduced sales and increased costs, impacting gross margin and net loss

  • HIGHER COSTS - Gross margin fell due to higher shipping, labour and production costs mainly driven by the temporary CFIA suspension and lower fixed cost absorption

  • REORGANIZATION COSTS - Employee termination and other reorganization costs contributed to wider net loss

data.title: Equifax Q1 revenue beats estimates on US mortgage activity

data.title: Space investment in first quarter hits record as SpaceX IPO buzz lifts sector
data.description:
Key points:
  • Average deal size nearly doubles, led by Saronic's $1.75 billion funding round
  • North America dominates funding, Europe and Asia also see strong investment growth
  • Capital increasingly targets in-space infrastructure like stations, data centers

By Akash Sriram

Global investment in space companies surged to a record in the first quarter of 2026, driven by larger late-stage financing and growing investor enthusiasm over SpaceX's public market debut, data from investment firm Seraphim Space showed on Tuesday.

Funding reached $7.95 billion during the quarter, nearly double the $3.93 billion in the previous three-month period, pushing the trailing 12-month investment to an all-time high of $18.8 billion.

Deal count also rose to 159 transactions, bringing the annual total to a record 654.

The increase in capital deployment was largely attributed to bigger cheque sizes rather than a sharp increase in deal volume, with average deal size climbing to $68 million from $35.1 million in fourth quarter. The largest transaction was U.S.-based Saronic's $1.75 billion round, one of the biggest space financings on record, the report said.

"The market today definitely feels 'risk-on' with capital moving quickly into perceived category leaders," said Lucas Bishop, investment associate at Seraphim Space, pointing to a convergence of tailwinds, including defense spending, renewed lunar ambitions and investor anticipation around SpaceX's IPO.

A SpaceX IPO could provide a landmark liquidity event for early investors and employees, while also creating a valuation benchmark, improving exit visibility for venture-backed space companies. Elon Musk's rocket maker ‌will host an analyst day ​on Tuesday, Reuters reported earlier this month.

North America accounted for roughly 70% of total funding in the first quarter, while Europe posted its strongest performance since 2022 and Asia contributed more than $1.2 billion.

Notably, investment has shifted beyond traditional satellite communications, with significantly more capital flowing into emerging segments such as in-space infrastructure, including space stations and data centers, reflecting a broadening of the sector's addressable market.

Recent developments have also highlighted continued momentum in satellite connectivity, with Amazon saying last week it would acquire Globalstar for $11.6 billion.

data.title: Sow Good to Acquire Nachu Graphite Project Holders From Ryzon in AUD150 Million Stock Deal
data.description:
Sow Good entered into a Share Purchase Agreement to acquire 100% of Uranex Tanzania and Magnis Technologies (Tanzania), which own the Nachu Graphite Project, from Ryzon. The AUD150 million (about US$107 million) consideration will be paid entirely in Sow Good common stock, adjusted for net debt and certain taxes, equating to approximately 334.2 million shares (or about 22.28 million after a 15-to-1 reverse split). A portion equal to AUD15 million will be held back as escrow via contingent value rights. Closing is subject to shareholder consent, Tanzanian regulatory approvals, Nasdaq listing approval of the shares, and other customary conditions, with a sunset date of Oct 15, 2026.

Agreement details:

  • Agreement type: Share Purchase Agreement for 100% acquisition of Uranex Tanzania and Magnis Technologies (Tanzania)
  • Counterparty: Ryzon Materials and sellers
  • Signed / Effective: Apr 20 2026 / same
  • Duration / Termination: Until closing; Sunset Oct 15 2026
  • Reason: Acquire Nachu Graphite Project to expand graphite supply and resources

Original SEC Filing: Sow Good Inc. [ SOWG ] - 8-K - Apr. 21, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: GE Aerospace Revenue Rises as Orders Surge
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

GE Aerospace posted higher first-quarter revenue on surging orders amid strong air travel and military demand.

The company reported net income from continuing operations of $1.93 billion, or $1.83 a share, compared with $1.97 billion, or $1.83 a share, the year prior.

Adjusted earnings were $1.86 a share. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected $1.60 a share.

Revenue rose 25% to $12.39 billion. Orders increased 87% to $23 billion. Commercial engines and services adjusted revenue was up 34%.

The company maintained its prior guidance, but said that it was trending toward the high end of its range. It expects fiscal-year adjusted earnings of $7.10 to $7.40 a share. Analysts see $7.49 a share.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: 3M posts Q1 revenue $6.03B, adjusted EPS $2.14 and adjusted operating margin 23.8%
data.description:
3M reported first-quarter 2026 results with GAAP revenue of $6.03 billion and GAAP operating margin of 23.2%. Adjusted results showed sales of $6.00 billion, adjusted operating margin of 23.8% and adjusted diluted EPS of $2.14. The company generated $574 million of operating cash flow and $541 million of adjusted free cash flow while reiterating full-year 2026 guidance.

Financial Highlights

  • Net sales (GAAP): $6,030 million for Q1 2026 (up 1.3% year‑on‑year).
  • Adjusted net sales (non‑GAAP): $6,003 million for Q1 2026 (adjusted sales change +3.9% YoY).
  • Operating income (GAAP): $1,397 million; GAAP operating margin 23.2% (up 230 bps YoY).
  • Adjusted operating income margin (non‑GAAP): 23.8% (up 30 bps YoY).
  • Adjusted diluted EPS (non‑GAAP): $2.14 (up 14% YoY); GAAP diluted EPS: $1.23 (down 40% YoY).
  • Cash flow: Net cash provided by operating activities $574 million; adjusted free cash flow $541 million.

Business Highlights

  • Segment revenue mix: Reportable segments generated $5,909 million in sales with Safety and Industrial at $2,930 million, Transportation and Electronics $1,848 million, and Consumer $1,131 million.
  • Segment performance: Safety and Industrial reported organic growth (3.2%); Transportation and Electronics and Consumer showed mixed organic trends with Transportation roughly flat and Consumer slightly negative organic sales.
  • Geographic trends: Strong growth in China (4.4% organic) and Europe, Middle East & Africa benefited from translation gains; Americas organic sales declined.
  • Corporate actions and restructuring: Company continues transformation program (incurring transformation costs) and reflected reclassification of manufactured PFAS products and certain litigation items into Corporate for clearer segment reporting.
  • Capital allocation and shareholder returns: Returned $2.4 billion to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases in the quarter; purchases of PPE were $225 million.

Original SEC Filing: 3M CO [ MMM ] - 8-K - Apr. 21, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: D.R. Horton Stock Rises on Earnings Beat. Have Home Builders Hit Bottom? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Shaina Mishkin

As the housing market limps along, analysts have become less enthused about D.R. Horton, the nation's largest home builder by market capitalization. The worst headwinds could abate this year even if earnings are soft, one analyst says.

D.R. Horton is set to report second quarter earnings on Tuesday before the market opens. According to consensus estimates by FactSet, the builder will report $7.55 billion in revenue and $2.13 a share. That would be down from $7.7 billion in revenue and $2.58 earnings per share in the year-ago period.

Builders have seen a bumpy start to the year. Prospective buyers were whiplashed by mortgage rates, which fell briefly below 6% this winter before jumping as high as 6.6% in late March, according to Mortgage News Daily. They have since mellowed to a recent 6.3%.

The swoon came after President Donald Trump said Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac would buy mortgage bonds, while the jump followed the 10-year Treasury yield's move higher as a result of the war in Iran.

"I don't think demand has been completely annihilated because of the war, but I'm sure it's been impacted," says John Lovallo, a UBS analyst covering home builders. "Consumer confidence has absolutely been impacted."

Analysts have become progressively more cautious on D.R. Horton. In the quarter ended March 31, 29% of analysts covering the stock rated it Buy, down from 41% one year prior, according to FactSet. The stock closed at $153.34 on Monday, its highest close since March 2, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Shares are up 6.5% this year.

Investors are most focused on finding a bottom for builders' margins, Lovallo says. Consensus estimates for D.R. Horton foresee a 19.1% home sales gross margin, narrower than the 21.8% the builder reported in the same quarter in 2025. Estimates are for 19,941 deliveries and 23,583 new orders.

Investors should keep their eyes on guidance, which could influence expectations for the company's third quarter. Analysts as of Monday afternoon expect 22,849 deliveries in the third quarter with a roughly 18% home sales gross margin.

If other builder earnings are any guide, investors watching D.R. Horton and PulteGroup, which is scheduled to report results on Thursday, can expect "relatively soft quarters" and lower outlooks, Lovallo says. That "doesn't sound like a great setup," he says. But "there's a view out there that we're getting closer to a bottom in the cycle," he adds.

Lovallo has a Buy on both stocks, he says. "If folks believe that the margins are going to bottom this year and consumer confidence will bottom this year — which I hope it does, because we're running at very low levels — then this group becomes very exciting," he says.

Write to Shaina Mishkin at shaina.mishkin@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US Reports Progress In Combating HIV Epidemic Globally
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - The U.S. Government has reported tremendous progress in reducing mother-to-child transmission of HIV, and in combating the epidemic as a whole across the world.

The U.S. Department of State has released PEPFAR program data covering the period of July 1, 2025, to September 31, 2025.

U.S. Government-supported programs provided anti-retroviral treatment for 20.6 million people living with HIV in more than 50 countries, stable from FY 2024 Q4.

PEPFAR initiated 103,000 pregnant and breastfeeding women to start pre-exposure prophylaxis to protect them and their babies from HIV-more than doubling the 43,000 pregnant and breastfeeding women on pre-exposure prophylaxis just one year ago.

The number of children on HIV treatment declined from 643,627 in 2022 to 508,703 in 2025 - representing year-over-year declines of 7 percent, 7 percent , and 9 percent respectively. This reflects tremendous progress in reducing mother-to-child transmission consistent with historical trends, according to a fact sheet published by the Department of State.

These declines are expected to continue, particularly with U.S. Government funding of Lenacapavir, which can further prevent mother-to-child transmission.

Positive HIV tests declined from 1,693,349 in 2022 to 1,136,488 in 2025 - representing year-over-year declines of 14 percent, 12 percent, and 11 percent respectively - reflecting progress in combating the HIV epidemic consistent with historical trends.

The State Department said this data does not reflect the impact of bilateral MOUs or the broader strategies being implemented under the America First Global Health Strategy.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Safran Faces More Challenging Aftermarket Business — Market Talk
data.description: French aerospace group Safran faces a more challenging aftermarket business, Jefferies analysts say. The short-lived reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway for fuel trade, gave some respite to civil aerospace groups, but the situation is likely to remain volatile and impact air traffic demand, the analysts say. Safran's first-quarter results, due April 23, should be solid and not impacted by the Middle East conflict, they say. Jefferies downgrades its rating of Safran stock to hold from buy, and cuts its target price to 310 euros from 350 euros. Shares are down 2.7% at 294.7 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: AI Remains a Glaring Problem for Apple. Why Cook's Successor Can Fix It. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Apple CEO Tim Cook is leaving on a high but with one huge issue hanging over the company — artificial intelligence. His successor John Ternus might be the right person to finally get the iPhone maker's AI era under way.

Cook can move to his new executive chairman role in September with some satisfaction. He has grown Apple's market valuation from around $350 billion to nearly $4 trillion, kept growing its iPhone base, and navigated U.S.-China tensions skillfully. But the company's failure to develop its own advanced AI is an issue.

While a relative lack of spending on AI means Apple hasn't faced the same cash-flow pressure as its rivals, it's also a big risk. The company has had to get Google to power an upgrade for its Siri digital assistant — an embarrassing change for Apple, which has prided itself on its ability to maintain strict control over its own platforms.

Given Apple's iPhone remains its profit engine, the biggest concern is if AI-powered hardware devices finally make inroads against smartphones. Meta Platforms looks to finally be taking smart glasses into the mainstream with the success of its Ray-Ban branded eyewear. ChatGPT-developer OpenAI acquired iPhone designer Jony Ive's hardware start-up for $6.5 billion last year for its own push into devices, although so far nothing has reached the market.

Therefore it should be a relief for Apple shareholders that the new CEO will be Ternus, the company's head of hardware engineering. He is expected to lead a wave of new products, with the headline releases widely reported to be a foldable smartphone and a range of smart glasses. The company needs a hit after the flop of its Vision Pro headset.

Apple still has a window to become the AI hardware leader but it won't last forever. Ternus will have his work cut out.

  • Adam Clark

Get more of the journalism you love. Choose Barron's as a preferred source in Google.

Apple's Cook to Step Down as CEO. It's a Very Different Tech Giant Now.

Apple's CEO Tim Cook knows all about replacing legends, having taken the helm of the tech giant following the departure of Steve Jobs in 2011. The 65-year-old is evolving to a new role as executive chairman of Apple's board, making way for executive John Ternus to become the new CEO.

  • Ternus is currently Apple's senior vice president of hardware engineering. Apple's market value was around $300 million when Cook became CEO, and now it's $4 trillion, thanks to the consumer tech ecosystem Cook established, and the recurring revenue it continues to generate.
  • Cook has also deftly navigated the complicated trade politics of President Donald Trump, the tech-skeptic policies of President Joe Biden, and the deeply suspicious government of China's Xi Jinping. Apple's surprise announcement came just days after it marked its 50th anniversary.
  • There are 2.5 billion Apple devices in use around the world, and the company is staking new claims in music, movies, and streaming media each year. Apple's logo is one of the most recognized, its customers are some of the most loyal, and its products are some of the most ubiquitous.
  • But Apple's grip on AI has been anything but secure. Its failure to establish a market-leading grip on the world's hottest, and most transformative, technology, will remain a key mark against Cook's legendary tenure. The company could set out a new AI strategy at its developer's conference this June.

What's Next: Before that, Apple will first post its fiscal second-quarter earnings on April 30. Analysts are looking for $57 billion in iPhone revenue, services growth of around 14%, and overall sales of around $110 billion.

  • Martin Baccardax and Angela Palumbo

Amazon Deepens Ties, Investment in AI Start-Up Anthropic

Amazon is deepening its ties to the AI start-up Anthropic, investing another $5 billion and possibly up to $20 billion longer-term. The deal will help quench Anthropic's thirst for the computing power needed for its AI models. For its part, Anthropic will buy billions of dollars of Amazon's AI products.

  • Anthropic, developer of the Claude large language model, will spend more than $100 billion over the next 10 years on Amazon's AWS artificial intelligence technologies, including current and future generations of Amazon's custom Trainium chips, tens of millions of Graviton CPU chips, and up to 5 gigawatts of capacity.
  • Amazon said AWS customers will be able to access Anthropic's full Claude platform from their existing AWS account, without needing additional credentials, contracts, or billing relationships. Anthropic said it uses AWS as its primary training and cloud provider for mission-critical workloads.
  • Anthropic recently said it has seen unprecedented demand growth and that its revenue run rate had exceeded $30 billion, up from $9 billion at the end of 2025. Rival AI lab OpenAI has said it is making $2 billion a month as the race for AI domination intensifies.
  • The two companies also announced a meaningful expansion of international inference in Asia and Europe to better serve Claude's growing international customer base. Amazon's additional investment comes on top of the $8 billion Amazon previously invested in Anthropic.

What's Next: Demand growth has its downsides. Anthropic said in an online post that fast growth is straining its infrastructure, which can affect reliability and performance for free and paid users. Anthropic also expanded a deal with Broadcom to access 3.5 gigawatts of Google's tensor processing units beginning in 2027.

  • Janet H. Cho

Importers Report Issues on First Day of Tariff Refund Portal

Companies were already reporting error messages and other issues after Customs & Border Protection launched its tariff refund portal that is supposed to allow importers to get money back after the Supreme Court struck down the Trump administration's global tariffs.

  • While some companies were able to file for a refund, others got error warnings such as, "The system is currently experiencing high volume, please try again." Others ran into issues trying to upload documents and businesses calling CBP to try to get answers weren't able to get through.
  • A CBP spokeswoman didn't directly address the error messages and recommended checking the agency's site periodically. She added that the agency has developed a new tool to efficiently process refunds and has issued guidance to the trade community to help them prepare to use the new tool.
  • Matthew Seligman, founder of Grayhawk Law, who advises small and medium-size businesses, said most of his clients were waiting to file in hopes of avoiding delays and errors. Seligman said the portal was built in about six weeks so it remains to be seen how well-functioning the system will be.
  • Analysts expect tariff refunds to take roughly 45 days or more. In its filing last week, the CBP said it can issue electronic refunds for about $127 billion, or 82% of the tariffs paid related to emergency powers. The Yale Budget Lab estimated that the IEEPA tariff revenue amounted to about $165 billion.

What's Next: More tariffs are on the way. Seeking to replace the levies struck down, the administration is turning to other legal authorities. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has said President Donald Trump hasn't decided whether to increase the temporary 10% global Section 122 tariff to 15%.

  • Reshma Kapadia

Alaska Air Suspends Guidance Amid Soaring Jet-Fuel Costs

It's a big week for airlines, with a flurry of earnings reports slated for release. Alaska Air Group reported a wider-than-expected first-quarter loss and suspended full-year guidance Monday, blaming rising fuel costs.

  • The carrier reported a first-quarter loss of $1.68 a share, widening from a loss of 77 cents a year ago. Sales increased 5% to $3.3 billion. Wall Street called for Alaska Air to post a first-quarter loss of $1.65 a share, with sales totaling $3.29 billion, according to FactSet.
  • Alaska Air said Monday that first-quarter fuel costs increased "materially due to elevated crude and refining prices," averaging $2.98 per gallon for the period. The company expects April fuel to be about $4.75 per gallon and that the average price of fuel in the second quarter will be $4.50 per gallon.
  • As a result of rising fuel costs, Alaska Air said it was suspending full-year guidance, noting that the war in the Middle East makes the second-quarter difficult to predict as well.
  • "Fuel remains the largest source of near--term uncertainty," Alaska Air said. Airline stocks have been hit by a spike in jet fuel prices during the Iran war, as closures to the Strait of Hormuz -- a maritime chokepoint which transports roughly 20% of the world's oil -- have caused a supply crunch.

What's Next: United Airlines is expected to release first-quarter earnings after the bell Tuesday. Southwest and American Airlines are expected to post their numbers later in the week. Investors will be looking out for the impact of higher fuel prices on the companies' bottom lines.

  • Kit Norton and Alex Kozul-Wright

Drought Conditions Spurring Worries About Higher Food Prices Drought conditions across the nation's growing regions are adding to the stresses facing U.S. farmers and raising concerns they could drive food prices even higher. David Ortega, a food economist and professor at Michigan State University, called current conditions "a perfect storm of many shocks."

  • The National Drought Mitigation Center's latest U.S. Drought Monitor from April 14 says 51% of U.S. states and Puerto Rico are in moderate drought or worse as spring planting season reaches its height. Farmers were already facing higher prices for fertilizer and diesel fuel because of the Iran war.
  • These higher costs come on top of trade tariffs and labor costs, said Ortega. While it's too early to say how much drought will affect food prices, agriculture is very dependent on weather and climate, Ortega said. Certain food prices are already elevated from a year ago.
  • The latest consumer price index, the first reading since the Iran war began, showed that grocery prices rose 1.9% in March from a year ago. Although food inflation has moderated from 2022, Ortega said prices are up about 30% from before the Covid-19 pandemic.
  • Bill Kirk, CEO of Weather Trends International, said the impact on food prices might not be as bad as feared. He said U.S. corn and soybean crops had record-shattering yields despite last year's drought, and this year is expected to be even wetter, eventually.

What's Next: In contrast to the colder-than-normal La Niña temperatures of the past six years that have brought drought to North and South America, an El Niño weather pattern will bring drought conditions to Southwest Asia and Australia, but wet conditions to the Americas, Kirk said.

  • Janet H. Cho
  • Newsletter edited by Liz Moyer, Patrick O'Donnell, Rupert Steiner

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: France's Engie discussing refund for offshore wind projects with Trump administration
data.description:
By Forrest Crellin

French utility Engie is in talks with the Trump administration about a possible refund for its offshore wind project leases amid the president's opposition to the technology, CEO Catherine MacGregor said on Tuesday.

Any deal would follow French oil major TotalEnergies redirecting nearly $1 billion (850 million euros) from offshore wind leases to U.S. oil and natural gas production.

"We'll see about these terms, an agreement is possible depending on the discussions," MacGregor said at a press breakfast.

"But honestly ... there's going to be a change in government, and I think (offshore wind permitting) comes back," she added.

Engie has paused three U.S. offshore wind projects in development and booked impairments for its joint venture Ocean Winds since Trump returned to the White House last year.

Trump has said he finds wind turbines ugly, costly and inefficient, and his administration has moved to increase domestic fossil fuel production.

"Economically and also in terms of public acceptance, I strongly believe in offshore wind power. Of course, you have to plan the projects well, you have to involve the fishermen," she said.

(1 euro = $1.1759)

data.title: German Financial Sentiment Slumps to Lowest Since 2022 on Energy Shock — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

Confidence among German investors this month sank to its weakest level in more than three years, as firms were shaken by the spike in energy prices from the conflict in the Middle East.

The ZEW Indicator of Economic Sentiment for Germany, which this month tracked the expectations of 192 analysts and investors at banks, insurance companies and other businesses, fell to minus 17.2 in April, compared with minus 0.5 in March.

The result marked a second negative reading since April last year and the lowest since December 2022. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected the headline reading to decline less sharply to minus 5.0.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has sent oil-and-gas prices soaring, putting particular strain on the German economy given its energy-intensive industrial sector. Brent-crude prices are up more than 30% since the first strikes on Iran at the end of February, while benchmark gas prices are more than 20% higher.

Annual inflation in Germany climbed to 2.8% in March, from 2.0% in February, according to EU-harmonized data. The European Central Bank is expected to keep rates on hold when it meets next week as policymakers gauge the impact of the war.

But the economic consequences of the Iran war for the German economy go far beyond price increases, ZEW President Achim Wambach said.

"Businesses are concerned about long-term shortages of energy supply, and this discourages investment and weakens the effect of government stimuli," he said.

The German government last year pledged more than $1 trillion in investments on defense and infrastructure, which had been hoped to inject fresh impetus into an economy that had flatlined since before the pandemic. However, official data showed production output has declined since the start of 2026.

Prospects for the chemical and pharmaceutical industries as well as for the steel and metal production sharply deteriorated, Wambach added, underlining the impact of the war on the industrial sector.

However, the latest set of purchasing managers' survey data for March suggests that Germany's services sector is bearing much of the brunt of a slowdown in private-sector activity in the country.

The ZEW indicator had been at a four-plus year high at the start of the year, as investors ramped up hopes of stimulus spending and having successfully managed much of the trade turmoil from the increases in U.S. tariffs.

"It has become clear that the optimism at the start of the year-that increased spending on defence and infrastructure would bolster a recovery-has turned into despair," Ankita Amajuri, Europe economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics, said in a note to clients.

The International Monetary Fund last week downgraded its expectations for the German economy, expecting just 0.8% growth this year from 1.1% it anticipated in January before the war upended forecasts.

Germany's government has also proposed nearly $2 billion in fuel-price relief to help consumers with energy costs.

However, ECB President Christine Lagarde in a speech Monday warned governments against being too helpful.

"When support is extended across the income distribution, it sustains demand that firms can then use to pass on higher costs--forcing monetary policy to tighten more than it otherwise would," she said in Berlin.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: Forestar edges past Q2 revenue estimates; narrows FY lot delivery view
data.description:
Overview

  • U.S. residential lot developer's fiscal Q2 revenue grew 7%, slightly beating analyst expectations

  • Adjusted EPS for fiscal Q2 rose to $0.63

  • Company narrowed fiscal 2026 lot delivery guidance to 14,000-14,500 lots

Outlook

  • Forestar updates fiscal 2026 lot delivery guidance to 14,000-14,500 lots, down from 14,000-15,000

  • Company maintains fiscal 2026 revenue guidance of $1.6 bln to $1.7 bln

  • Forestar says affordability constraints and cautious consumer sentiment continue to impact new home sales pace

Result Drivers

  • HIGHER TRACT SALES - Increased tract sales and other revenue offset declines in residential lot sales

  • FEWER LOTS SOLD - Company sold 2,938 lots in Q2, down 14% from 3,411 in prior year quarter, citing ongoing affordability constraints and cautious consumer sentiment

  • HIGHER AVERAGE SALES PRICE - Average sales price per lot increased to $112,800 from $101,700 in prior year quarter

data.title: Palm Oil Rises, Supported by Rival Oils Amid Iran War — Market Talk
data.description: Palm oil ended higher. Prices were supported by gains in rival oils as ongoing uncertainty surrounding the prospects for talks to end the Iran conflict keeps energy markets firm, Kenanga Futures analysts say in a note. However, upsides may be capped by expectations of rising domestic production during the peak output season. Kenanga Futures sees support for prices at 4,465 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,580 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for June delivery closed 63 ringgit higher at 4,561 ringgit a ton. (jason.chau@wsj.com)
